description: Demo index generated from education-only finance notes. Replace with chunks extracted from PDFs in production.

chunks.title: Systematic Investment Plan
chunks.text: A Systematic Investment Plan, or SIP, is a method of investing a fixed amount at regular intervals. It can support investing discipline and reduce the stress of trying to time the market. SIP outcomes depend on the chosen asset, time horizon, market risk, costs, and investor behaviour.

chunks.title: Emergency Fund
chunks.text: An emergency fund is money kept aside for unexpected events such as job loss, medical expenses, urgent travel, or repairs. Many education frameworks discuss keeping several months of essential expenses in liquid and relatively safe instruments before taking higher investment risk.

chunks.title: EMI and Income Capacity
chunks.text: EMI affordability should be assessed from net take-home income after essential expenses, rent, existing EMIs, insurance, dependents, and emergency fund contributions. As an educational rule of thumb, total EMIs above 30 to 40 percent of take-home income can become stressful for many households, especially when income is unstable or dependents are present.

chunks.title: Health Insurance Cover
chunks.text: Health insurance needs depend on city, age, family size, employer cover, existing illnesses, dependents, hospital costs, and emergency savings. Education frameworks usually treat health insurance as protection before investing. A young single person, a couple, and a family with children or parents can need very different cover levels, so compare likely hospital costs and avoid relying only on employer insurance.

chunks.title: Diversification
chunks.text: Diversification means spreading money across assets, sectors, or instruments so that one bad outcome does not dominate the full plan. Diversification can reduce concentration risk, but it does not eliminate market risk, liquidity risk, inflation risk, or behaviour risk.

chunks.title: Risk Capacity
chunks.text: Risk capacity depends on age, income stability, dependents, emergency fund, debt burden, goal horizon, liquidity needs, and ability to handle losses. Risk capacity is different from risk preference, which is how comfortable a person feels with volatility.

chunks.title: Compounding
chunks.text: Compounding means earning returns on earlier returns. The main drivers are time, consistency, reinvestment, and the actual return earned. Compounding is powerful over long periods, but it does not guarantee returns and can be interrupted by withdrawals, poor asset choice, high costs, or market losses.
